content.title: Measure word 个 in ordering
content.body:
When ordering quantities, you need the measure word 个 (ge) between the number and the noun.

**Structure:** number + 个 + [noun]

Examples:
- 两个 (liǎng ge) — two (of them)
- 三个苹果 (sān ge píngguǒ) — three apples
- 我要一个。(Wǒ yào yí ge.) — I want one.

Note: Use 两 (liǎng) not 二 (èr) for "two" when counti

content.title: Asking price with 多少钱
content.body:
To ask "How much?" in Chinese, use 多少钱? (duōshao qián?). This literally means "how much money?"

**Usage:**
- 这个多少钱? (Zhège duōshao qián?) — How much is this?
- 那个多少钱? (Nàge duōshao qián?) — How much is that?

You can point at items while asking this question. The shopkeeper will respond with a numb

content.title: Numbers in prices — Review
content.body:
Understanding numbers 1-100 is crucial for prices. Quick review of key numbers:

**1-10:** 一 (yī), 二 (èr), 三 (sān), 四 (sì), 五 (wǔ), 六 (liù), 七 (qī), 八 (bā), 九 (jiǔ), 十 (shí)

**Teens:** 十 + number (e.g., 十五 shíwǔ = 15)

**Tens:** number + 十 (e.g., 二十 èrshí = 20)

**Compound:** tens + ones (e.g., 二十五

content.title: Understanding total prices
content.body:
When buying multiple items, the shopkeeper will state the total. Listen for:

**Individual prices then total:**
- "这个五块,那个十块,一共十五块。" (Zhège wǔ kuài, nàge shí kuài, yígòng shíwǔ kuài.)
- "This is 5 yuan, that is 10 yuan, altogether 15 yuan."

**一共** (yígòng) means "altogether" or "in total." It signa

content.title: Complete restaurant ordering
content.body:
**Dialogue: Ordering at a casual restaurant**

Server: 你好! 要什么? (Nǐ hǎo! Yào shénme?)  
Customer: 我要一个汉堡。(Wǒ yào yí ge hànbǎo.)  
Server: 好。还要吗? (Hǎo. Hái yào ma?)  
Customer: 还要一杯水。多少钱? (Hái yào yì bēi shuǐ. Duōshao qián?)  
Server: 汉堡二十五块,水不要钱。一共二十五块。(Hànbǎo èrshíwǔ kuài, shuǐ búyào qián. Yígòng è

content.title: 我要 — "I want"
content.body:
The phrase 我要 (wǒ yào) means "I want" and is the most direct way to order or request something in Chinese. It's perfectly acceptable in shops and restaurants.

**Structure:** 我要 + [thing]

Examples:
- 我要这个。(Wǒ yào zhège.) — I want this one.
- 我要水。(Wǒ yào shuǐ.) — I want water.
- 我要两个。(Wǒ yào liǎng g

content.title: 谢谢 — "Thank you"
content.body:
谢谢 (xièxie) means "thank you" and is essential in shopping interactions. Both customers and service workers use it throughout a transaction.

**Common usage:**
- After receiving something: 谢谢。(Xièxie.)
- After being told information: 谢谢。(Xièxie.)
- When leaving: 谢谢。(Xièxie.)

You may also hear 谢谢你 (

content.title: Multiple items ordering
content.body:
When ordering several items, you can list them using 我要 once, or repeat it, or use 还要 for additional items.

**Options:**
- List: 我要这个、那个、一个苹果。(Wǒ yào zhège, nàge, yí ge píngguǒ.)
- Repeat: 我要这个。我要那个。(Wǒ yào zhège. Wǒ yào nàge.)
- Add: 我要这个。还要那个。(Wǒ yào zhège. Hái yào nàge.)

All three are acceptabl

content.title: Complete shop transaction dialogue
content.body:
**Dialogue: Buying fruit at a market stall**

Customer: 你好! (Nǐ hǎo!)  
Vendor: 你好! (Nǐ hǎo!)  
Customer: 这个苹果多少钱? (Zhège píngguǒ duōshao qián?)  
Vendor: 五块一个。(Wǔ kuài yí ge.)  
Customer: 我要三个。(Wǒ yào sān ge.)  
Vendor: 好。还要吗? (Hǎo. Hái yào ma?)  
Customer: 不要了。多少钱? (Búyào le. Duōshao qián?)  
Vend

content.title: 不客气 — "You're welcome"
content.body:
不客气 (bú kèqi) is the standard response to 谢谢. It means "you're welcome" or literally "don't be polite."

**Usage:**
- Customer: 谢谢。(Xièxie.)
- Shopkeeper: 不客气。(Bú kèqi.)

This phrase closes the courtesy exchange. You might also hear 不用谢 (búyòng xiè) which means "no need to thank," but 不客气 is more co

content.title: 还要 — "Also want"
content.body:
还要 (hái yào) means "also want" and is used to add items to your order.

**Usage:**
- First item: 我要这个。(Wǒ yào zhège.)
- Adding more: 还要那个。(Hái yào nàge.)

Alternatively, the shopkeeper might ask "还要吗?" (Hái yào ma?) — "Do you want anything else?"

content.title: Handling misunderstanding
content.body:
**When you don't understand:**

Vendor: 这个三十八块。(Zhège sānshíbā kuài.)  
Customer: 对不起,多少? (Duìbuqǐ, duōshao?)  
Vendor: 三十八。三十八块。(Sānshíbā. Sānshíbā kuài.)  
Customer: 好,谢谢。(Hǎo, xièxie.)

**Key phrase:** 对不起,多少? (Duìbuqǐ, duōshao?) — "Sorry, how much?"

You can also say 请再说一次 (qǐng zài shuō yí cì)

content.title: Listening comprehension — fast transaction
content.body:
**Rapid café exchange (typical speed):**

Customer: 我要一杯咖啡。(Wǒ yào yì bēi kāfēi.)  
Barista: 二十块。(Èrshí kuài.)  
Customer: 给你。(Gěi nǐ.)  
Barista: 谢谢! (Xièxie!)  
Customer: 谢谢! (Xièxie!)

This represents a very quick transaction as it actually happens — minimal words, fast pace. Notice both parties

content.title: Price negotiation — accepting the price
content.body:
In most modern shops and restaurants, prices are fixed. When told the price, you typically:

**1. Accept silently** — just hand over money  
**2. Acknowledge:** 好 (hǎo) — "OK"  
**3. Pay:** 给你 (gěi nǐ) — "Here you are"

In some markets, negotiation is possible, but that's beyond HSK 1 scope. For beg

content.title: Price responses — 块 (kuài)
content.body:
When someone tells you a price, they'll use 块 (kuài) or 元 (yuán) for yuan (the Chinese currency). 块 is the colloquial, everyday term used in shops and restaurants.

**Examples:**
- 五块。(Wǔ kuài.) — Five yuan.
- 十块。(Shí kuài.) — Ten yuan.
- 三十块。(Sānshí kuài.) — Thirty yuan.

In formal contexts or writ

content.title: 这个 and 那个 in ordering
content.body:
When ordering, you'll often point and say 这个 (zhège) "this one" or 那个 (nàge) "that one" rather than naming the item.

**In practice:**
- 我要这个。(Wǒ yào zhège.) — I want this one.
- 那个多少钱? (Nàge duōshao qián?) — How much is that one?

This is extremely common in shops and restaurants with visible items

content.title: Polite particle 请 (qǐng)
content.body:
请 (qǐng) means "please" and can make requests more polite. It comes before the verb.

**Structure:** 请 + [verb phrase]

Examples:
- 请给我。(Qǐng gěi wǒ.) — Please give me.
- 请问 (Qǐngwèn) — May I ask...

While 我要 is perfectly acceptable in ordering, adding 请 makes your request more courteous. However, i

content.title: Responding to "还要吗?"
content.body:
When a shopkeeper asks "还要吗?" (Hái yào ma? — "Want anything else?"), you have two options:

**If adding more:**  
还要[item]. (Hái yào [item].) — "I also want [item]."

**If finished:**  
不要了,谢谢。(Búyào le, xièxie.) — "No more, thanks."  
OR simply: 不要了。(Búyào le.)

The 了 (le) particle indicates change

content.title: Variation: 要 vs 想要
content.body:
Both 要 (yào) and 想要 (xiǎng yào) can express wanting something:

**要 (yào):** Direct, decisive — "want/will have"  
**想要 (xiǎng yào):** Softer, "would like"

In ordering contexts, 我要 is standard and perfectly polite. 想要 is less common in quick transactions but fine to use. Some speakers prefer 想要 as

content.title: Combining 请 with requests
content.body:
Using 请 (qǐng) with other phrases creates polite requests:

**Common combinations:**
- 请给我这个。(Qǐng gěi wǒ zhège.) — Please give me this.
- 请问,多少钱? (Qǐngwèn, duōshao qián?) — Excuse me, how much?

请问 (qǐngwèn) literally means "please ask" but functions as "excuse me" when asking questions. It's more

content.title: Basic ordering sequence
content.body:
A typical simple ordering sequence follows this pattern:

**1. Order:** 我要[item]. (Wǒ yào [item].)  
**2. Confirm/add:** 还要[item]吗? (Hái yào [item] ma?)  
**3. Ask price:** [Item]多少钱? ([Item] duōshao qián?)  
**4. State price:** [Number]块。([Number] kuài.)  
**5. Payment exchange:** 给你。(Gěi nǐ.)  
**

content.title: 给你 — "Here you are"
content.body:
给你 (gěi nǐ) literally means "give you" but functions as "here you are" when handing something to someone. Both customers and shopkeepers use this phrase.

**When to use it:**
- When handing over money: 给你。(Gěi nǐ.)
- When giving items: 给你。(Gěi nǐ.)
- When passing something: 给你水。(Gěi nǐ shuǐ.) — Here
